name: A Cappella
description:
Georgia Tech A Cappella includes Nothin' But Treble, GT's premier all-upper voices a cappella group, and Sympathetic Vibrations, GT's premier all-male a cappella group.

Nothin’ But Treble, Georgia Tech’s premier all-upper voices a cappella group, was founded in 1998 by Dipika Jain as part of the Georgia Tech A Cappella Club. The group is made up of a variety of Georgia Tech students from Engineering and Biology to STAC majors. There are typically 12 to 16 active members ranging from Freshmen to Graduate students. All of the music in Nothin’ But Treble’s growing repertoire is arranged by a current or former member of the group. We cover a wide variety of genres of music from pop to jazz to 80s and more! We rehearse twice a week and perform at various venues and events. In the past we have performed at an Elite 8 March Madness game, the Variety Playhouse in Little 5 Points, for College of Management events, and opened for Nappy Roots, Bubba Sparxxx, and Crime Mob. We’ve also performed annually at Georgia Tech's Commencement, Tech sports games, and numerous Tech philanthropy events. Nothin' but Treble has also competed in the ICAAs (the competition in Pitch Perfect) numerous times, most recently in Spring 2023. We will soon be holding auditions for Fall 2025! Fill out this form to sign up for auditions. We're so excited to hear you sing! Website: https://www.nothinbuttreblegt.com/

Sympathetic Vibrations (SympVibes) is an all-male a cappella group founded in the Fall of 1997 by James Clawson. We usually have 12-15 active members as well as some members who are on co-op rotations or other various diversions. We rehearse three times a week and sing music arranged by members and alumni of the group. We perform at various events on Georgia Tech’s campus, in Atlanta, and across the state of Georgia. We also hold a few of our own concerts at various campus venues each semester. In 2017, we competed in The International Championship of Collegiate A Cappella (ICCA), winning 1st place in South Quarterfinals and 2nd place in the South Semifinals. Website: http://sympvibes.com/
summary: Georgia Tech A Cappella includes Nothin' But Treble, GT's premier women's a cappella group, and Sympathetic Vibrations, GT's premier all-male a cappella group.

name: ABLE Alliance
description:
The ABLE Alliance is a student organization dedicated to advocating for individuals with disabilities at Georgia Tech.

Our Linktree contains access to our GroupMe, Instagram, LinkedIn, and Youtube. Our socials are free to join, and can be used to ask any questions/keep up with current events as we make our way forward: Linktree Link
summary: .The ABLE Alliance aims to connect students at Georgia Tech and build community while connecting them with career opportunities.

name: Table Tennis Association
description:
GTTTA’s mission is to provide an open, friendly, and competitive environment for members of the Georgia Tech community to improve and compete in the awesome sport of table tennis. Whether you are someone who has extensive experience or are just starting out, we hope to provide a community that can support your growth! New members should pay the $30 semesterly dues and visit our website to join the Discord.

 

 

All updates on the club including practice times can be found at our website: http://tta.gtorg.gatech.edu/.  Note that any practice day can have open play, but priority for space will be given to training and league. League Night is a weekly in-club round robin tournament, where members are placed into groups of 4-6 members and play round robins matches. These match scores are used to calculate our "Official Club Ratings", which can be found on the GTTTA website. Information on League can be found here: https://tta.gtorg.gatech.edu/league

In additional to league nights, we regularly send members to participate in tournaments ranging from individual events hosted by USATT or team events representing Georgia Tech at NCTTA tournaments.

 

Join our slack for easier communication and updates at: https://join.slack.com/t/gttta/shared_invite/zt-16popxcj5-zyuMkmd4ZeLzuJIrqWmgtg
summary: GTTTA’s mission is to provide an open, friendly, and competitive environment for members of the Georgia Tech community to improve and compete in the awesome sport of table tennis.

name: Mock Trial
description:
Check out our website www.georgiatechmocktrial.com and stay updated through our Instagram page @gtmocktrial

Mock trial is a competitive student-run organization focused on teaching students about legal advocacy and the legal system. We field four competitive teams and participate in simulated trials hosted by the American Mock Trial Association. Our students act as both attorneys and witnesses on all sides of the case, develop case theories and witness characters, and handle rules of evidence and objection arguments. We travel around the country to compete in invitational and regional tournaments against other universities. We even host our own tournament, the Ramblin' Wreck Invitational!

Mock Trial is not just for pre-law students, it is for everyone looking to gain a meaningful experience in college. We meet Tuesdays and Thursdays from 6:30 to 8:30 P.M.
summary: Mock Trial provides an opportunity for students to learn about the American legal system. We field four competitive teams and participate in simulated trials hosted by the American Mock Trial Association. See www.georgiatechmocktrial.com for more info!

name: FreShGA
description:
The focus of FreShGA is to not only build the leadership skills of Georgia Tech’s future leaders but to also foster friendships and memories that will last well beyond the freshman year. FreShGA members learn how to become effective leaders within both SGA and the larger Georgia Tech campus, and former members have gone on to become SGA President and have served on various executive boards in organizations across campus.

Recruitment Video!

First-Year students are invited to apply to FLOs like FreShGA via the FLO Common Application which opens each fall. Please visit the FLO website for more information.

name: Marine Robotics Group
description:
Marine Robotics Group is an organization dedicated to student-led exploratory robotics. We participate in various maritime robotics challenges, such as RoboSub and RobotX, and are working to send an autonomous boat across the Atlantic Ocean for the Microtransat Challenge.

We accept members of all majors, and aim to cultivate skills and assign work flexibly according to member interest. Projects include electrical system development, robotics software development, and mechanical and structures work. Checkout our website for more details!

Click the link below to visit our website and send us an email or fill out our interest form. Keep an eye out for open lab hours! We'll get back to you with a Teams and Discord invite as soon as possible.
summary: Sending robots into the blue - both exploratory and competitive. Join us in our attempt to cross the Atlantic and developing high performance autonomous vehicles.

name: Little Einsteins Organization
description:
Mission Statement: Little Einsteins Organization (LEO) is a chartered Georgia Tech campus organization that conducts and creates Science, Technology, Engineering and Math (STEM) focused activities with children in Atlanta.

 

LEO works with 150+ children at numerous flagship organizations in Atlanta multiple times a month. Each semester, we also assemble 700+ STEM Activity Kits for the Fulton County Libraries.

We currently have 250+ members with 2,000+ followers on our official Instagram page.

Membership is open to ALL undergraduate and graduate Georgia Tech students.

No special skills are required.

Transportation is provided to and from events. 

 

Follow us @gatech_leo on Instagram to see all our STEM Experiments.

Join our Slack to stay up to date on our latest announcements.

Fill out the membership form to attend off-campus events.
summary: The Little Einsteins Organization conducts and creates Science, Technology, Engineering, and Math (STEM) focused educational activities with 150+ children each week in partnership with numerous flagship organizations throughout Atlanta.

name: Chabad Jewish Student Group
description:
Chabad strives to be the go-to place for Jewish students at Georgia Tech. It provides a warm and vibrant environment for students to experience and learn about Jewish tradition and culture, and of course, to simply socialize with other Jews!

Chabad hosts:
Weekly Shabbat Dinners
Weekly Kosher BBQ's
Celebrations and Services for all Jewish Holidays
Sinai Scholars Society & Learning opportunities

name: Satrangi
description:
ATL Satrangi is the premier all-girls Bollywood fusion dance team from Atlanta, Georgia. They compete on the national level with styles including contemporary, Indian classical, bhangra, hip-hop and other Western styles. They strive to share their passion for dance and women empowerment both on and off stage. They perform sets that artfully combines Eastern and Western dance styles, truly blends each teammates unique dance background, and tells a captivating story. In the past, our team has traveled and competed in Atlanta, Minneapolis, Los Angeles, Cincinnati, Austin, DC, and many more. Come join our family!

 

Check out our 2024-25 season's set!

 

Check us out on Instagram @atl_satrangi

name: Student Center
description: The Student Center & Stamps Student Center Commons offers irresistible facilities, essential services, and life-shaping programs to the Georgia Tech Community Located in the heart of campus, the center offers 14 meeting rooms with seating for 12 to 500, a full-service post office, information desk, automatic teller machines, theater, ballroom, and a computer lab. As the central hub for campus life, the Student Center provides a complete range of social, artistic, cultural, and recreational activities for both students and the campus community to enjoy. With eight bowling lanes, eight billiards tables, a video game lounge, darts, and more at discounted rates, Tech Rec gives Georgia Tech a place to relax and have fun. Paper & Clay, the campus art studio, is equipped with everything the campus community needs to explore its creative side, including pottery, sewing, stained glass, and more.

name: Yellow Jacket Roller Derby
description:
Yellow Jacket Roller Derby at Georgia Tech is an open gender collegiate league for people interested in learning how to play roller derby! Our mission is to provide a welcoming environment and inclusive community for members to learn to play and appreciate flat track roller derby while promoting physical strength and fitness.

Watch a Video about Yellow Jacket Roller Derby Here!

See our updated events, attend one of our info sessions, and sign up for our email list by reaching out to yjrdofficers@lists.gatech.edu (:

--

Do I need to know how to skate to join?

Nope! We'll teach you everything you need to know from lacing your skates, to learning how to fall (correctly) and being the best skater you can be!

 

What is roller derby?

Roller derby is a competitive full-body contact sport. It's played on a flat elliptical track, and players wear quad roller skates and protective padding (helmets, mouth guards, elbow and knee pads, wrist guards). We play under the Women's Flat Track Derby Association (WFTDA) rules. 

 

How do you win?

There are two positions you can play in roller derby: Blocker (defense) or jammer (offense). Five players from each team are on the track at a time. Four are blockers and one is the jammer. The jammer is designated by a star on their helmet and is the only one who can score points for the team. The jammer scores one point for each opposing player they pass. The blockers from the other team work as a defensive line to stop the jammer from passing them and scoring points. The team with most points at the end of the bout (what we call a game) wins.

 

How do you play?

At the start of a round, called a "jam", the jammers from each team line up behind the jam line while the blockers line up in front of it. When the whistle blows, the jammers attempt to get through all the blockers, called the "pack". While neither jammer can score points on their initial pass through the pack, the first jammer to pass every opposing blocker the second time through gets "lead" status. This means that they can end the jam at any time they want by "calling it off" (signaled by the lead jammer gesturing from their helmet to their hips repeatedly). The blockers attempt to hold the opposing jammer for as long as possible while helping their own jammer get through the pack. Each jam lasts two minutes, unless called off by the lead jammer, and a game is broken into two thirty minute segments. For more detailed info check out the news article on our Engage!

 

What's the risk?

Roller derby has some inherent hazards associated with it, but safety is always first. Safety gear consists of knee pads, elbow pads, wrist guards, a helmet, and a mouth guard. Before you are allowed to play, you must first learn (and prove you've learned) basic skating skills to ensure you won't hurt yourself or others. Don't worry, we'll teach you! There are also a lot of rules in place to make sure everyone stays safe. Penalties are issued for numerous grievances, like elbowing or tripping, and these are all to keep the game clean and reduce risk of injury.

 

How to Get Involved

Feel free to message us or email at yjrdofficers@groups.gatech.edu. We can add you to our email list for consistent league information straight to your inbox. We host an open enrollment period for the first month of every fall and spring semester to allow new skaters to get introduced to roller derby. There is no experience necessary, no commitment required, and we provide skates and gear on a first come first serve basis.
summary: Yellow Jacket Roller Derby is a co-ed collegiate league for GT students interested in learning how to play roller derby! No experience required. We have an open enrollment period at the start of each fall and spring semester.

name: Debate Team
description:
Overview:

The debate team here at Georgia Tech aims to provide a welcoming and friendly environment for debaters of all levels to come together and improve their skill set. Whether you’re new to debate or have been debating for years, we’d love to have you join us! 

 

We mainly practice four different styles of debate - BP/WUDC, NPDA, IPDA, and Policy - and you can choose a style that suits your schedule and goals. BP/WUDC, IPDA, and NPDA consist of impromptu debate rounds, while policy requires extensive research and preparation. Through debate, you gain crucial skills such as critical thinking and reasoning, public speaking, rhetoric, and teamwork. Debate is also a fun channel for discussions regarding contemporary socio-political topics and themes.

 

We attend tournaments in a variety of debating formats both online and in-person. Our team has travelled everywhere from Nashville to Berkeley to Washington DC to attend prestigious tournaments, and even made the open finals at Vanderbilt InterVarsity! Due to a partnership with the Ivan Allen College, tournament travel costs are free to club members. 

 

Here at Georgia Tech Debate our goal is to help improve these universal debate skills through practice in a low-stress environment. At our weekly two-hour practices, we usually do a fun intro activity or mini-lesson on a debate topic/technique before splitting into teams and doing a scrimmage debate round. We usually debate against each other, but we do also have scrimmages with our collaborating debate teams at other institutions. After the round, debaters receive constructive feedback in order to improve their skills.

 

In addition to debate, we also support speech events and tournaments! For speech events ranging from impromptu speaking to extemp to oratory, we are happy to provide you feedback and support, as well as the ability to attend competitions.

 

No dues are required.

How to get involved:

Step #1 - Go to linktr.ee/debateteamgt

Step #2 - Follow the links to join our Membership Roster and our Discord. 

Step #3 - Start attending meetings!
summary: Georgia Tech's Debate Team provides an inclusive environment to learn and grow as a debater and public speaker. No prior experience is necessary!

name: Civic Engagement
description:
Civic Engagement at GT engages with communities on a local, national, and global scale. Whether you are a student, faculty or staff member, or community partner, Civic Engagement is ready to support your community partnership and service experience!

 

Broadly defined, civic engagement encompasses the numerous ways in which students, faculty, and staff promote positive social change. Opportunities to engage with community partners exist in co-curricular settings, academic based community service courses, and departmental sponsored outreach programs. Opportunities exist on a spectrum of service and participants are encouraged to match their interests and availability to the community need as appropriate along this spectrum. Projects range from direct service to awareness/advocacy to philanthropy. The frequency of service experiences includes options such as one-day service projects, on-going commitments, immersion trips, and semester-long courses with academic-based community service embedded in the curriculum. The types of issues addressed also vary as does the location of the projects available. Civic Engagement applies a student-centered approach to developing and clarifying identity, to understanding others, and to promoting social change.

 

Organizations and Programs in Civic Engagement: 

Alternative Service Breaks (ASB)

First-Year Servant Leadership Organization (FSLO)

Jumpstart

Mobilizing Opportunities for Volunteer Experiences (MOVE)
summary: Civic Engagement at GT engages with communities on a local, national, and global scale. Whether you are a student, faculty or staff member, or community partner, Civic Engagement is ready to support your community partnership and service experience!

name: Diversity in Business
description:
Welcome to Diversity in Business! We are a student organization that promotes and highlights diversity within our student community and the broader Business world by hosting networking and learning events with prominent companies. All of our events and organization updates can be found on our social media pages. 

Instagram: @gtdiversityinbusiness
summary: Welcome to Diversity in Business! We are a student organization within the Scheller College of Business that promotes and highlights diversity in the Business world. Our bi-weekly meetings provide members opportunities to learn and network.

name: Orchestra Club
description:
The School of Music currently offers two orchestral ensembles: the Georgia Tech Symphony Orchestra (GTSO), a full orchestra, and Concert Orchestra (CO), an intermediate string orchestra. GTSO is under the direction of Professor Chaowen Ting, who is also the Orchestra Club advisor, while CO is under the direction of Professor Timothy Verville. Each ensemble maintains a membership of approximately 70-90 students. In addition to concerts throughout the semester, the orchestra program provides access to master classes, professional engagements, and recording opportunities. Members enjoy social aspects through informal dinners and parties. Although not required to join the club, students who would like to perform in the orchestras are encouraged to follow the audition process. Semester dues are currently free of cost!

 

GTSO performs 4-6 concerts in an academic year. Concerts and repertoire expose participants to a variety of traditional and contemporary orchestral literature. Participation in GTSO broadens students’ skills and experience in performing and fosters understanding and appreciation of great music. Recent works performed include Mahler's Symphony No. 1, Rachmaninoff's Piano Concerto No. 3, and Rimsky-Korsakov's Scheherazade. GTSO Rehearsals are during TR 12:30-1:45 pm, with occasional evening rehearsals.

 

CO is an intermediate orchestra, providing players a supportive environment in which players develop personal string techniques and ensemble skills through practicing, rehearsing, and coaching. Players are exposed to classical string repertoire as well as modern and pop music. Concert Orchestra meets during TR 9:30-10:45am.

 

Orchestra Club aims to bridge musical passion with community engagement, fostering an inclusive space for both musicians and audiences alike, and bringing people together through group outings, social events, and exclusive performance opportunities.
summary: Orchestra Club is a fun and welcoming environment open to those who are interested in learning more about orchestra or orchestral music ranging from newcomers, enthusiasts, or performers in the GT orchestras. Come join us today!

name: Epic Intentions
description:
Epic Intentions is an interdisciplinary society of Georgia Tech volunteers whose purpose is to aid local non-profits, social enterprises, and civic-minded entrepreneurs through service-oriented design projects. Our goal is to leverage the unique skills of Georgia Tech students to give back to the community by solving problems for local non-profits. Our structure benefits both students and our partners. Students are able to leverage their unique technical skills through hands on, multidisciplinary projects that solve real world problems. Our partners are able to receive innovative technical solutions without needing an extensive staff or budget diverted away from their primary objectives.

To learn more about our nonprofit partners and other application guidelines, please click here! We'd love to have you join us to make impact on the Atlanta community this semester.
summary: Epic Intentions is an interdisciplinary society of Georgia Tech volunteers whose purpose is to aid local non-profits, social enterprises, and civic-minded entrepreneurs through service-oriented design projects.Our goal is to leverage the unique skills ...

The Georgia Tech John H. Martinson Honors Program (JMHP) fosters curiosity, creativity, and connection—to the JMHP, Georgia Tech, and communities beyond. JMHP students are encouraged to pursue their interests across disciplinary boundaries and the boundary between theory and practice. In advancing its mission, the JMHP enhances the capacity of our highly motivated students and future alumni to advance the Georgia Tech mission of “Progress and Service.”

The Georgia Tech John H. Martinson Honors Program (JMHP) advances its mission by the combined and mutually supportive elements of a living learning community. JMHP students live and learn together—inside and beyond the classroom—and create and sustain a JMHP community extending from the first year to graduation and beyond.
summary: The Georgia Tech John H. Martinson Honors Program (JMHP) fosters curiosity, creativity, and connection. JMHP students are encouraged to pursue their interests across disciplinary boundaries and the boundary between theory and practice.

SOFT is an organization for furries at Georgia Tech. We exist to provide for coordination of social activities and to promote furry interests, as well as to maintain a positive and accepting environment for any who would join us.

We meet every Friday!

name: Chinese Christian Fellowship
description:
We love the Lord Jesus Christ. Our community joins together as His messengers to share the good news about Jesus with students at Georgia Institute of Technology. Jesus said, “I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.”(John 10:10) As we choose to follow Him, we are experiencing the love of Jesus and growing our love and faith in Him day by day.


As a community, we gather once a week at Georgia Tech Exhibition Hall or Student Center to share our lives, engage in prayers, and study the bible in small student-led groups. We come together to worship God and listen to the teachings from the Scripture. We warmly and wholeheartedly welcome anyone who wants to seek Him or has already received Him to join us in fellowship. So we may enjoy the riches of Christ together, and experience His power of bringing love, healing, life, and hope to our lives. College is unique in our life. Explore and cherish the time to grow in faith in God's presence. As a community, we want to “encourage one another and build one another up” (1 Thess. 5:11)

 

You can join us by emailing student leaders or registering on our page on the official website. If you have any questions, please contact Haichuan (Walter) Wang (walwan@gatech.edu), our supervisor Alan Lin (alanlins@gmail.com) or our faculty advisor Prof. Yixiao Wang (ywang4372@gatech.edu).

 

Here is the fellowship tentative schedule for Spring 2026.
summary: In response to God's love, grace and truth: The Purpose of the Chapter is to establish and advance at Georgia Institute of Technology witnessing communities of students and faculty who follow Jesus as Savior and Lord: growing in love for God, God's Work.

name: African Student Association
description:
The African Student Association (ASA) at Georgia Tech is dedicated to fostering a vibrant community that embraces African culture and promotes cross-cultural engagement. Our mission is to unite African students on campus and connect with individuals passionate about African heritage. We aim to facilitate meaningful interactions among students of African descent or with ties to African nations, as well as those interested in African affairs.

Throughout the academic year, we curate a diverse range of events that celebrate our rich culture and raise awareness about current happenings in Africa. From cultural showcases to educational seminars, ASA provides a platform for dialogue and celebration.

For the 2025-2026 academic year, we are excited to announce our biweekly General Body meetings and alternating events and socials. Stay connected with ASA by following us on Instagram @gt_asa and join us in exploring the beauty and diversity of Africa!
summary: The African Student Association (ASA) at Georgia Tech is a cultural and social organization committed to the integration of African students on campus as well as engaging other individuals with an interest in African culture. We strive to promote inter...

name: Ignite Summer Programs
description:
Ignite your Georgia Tech experience by applying to one of our First Year Summer Launch Programs! All first year summer students enter Georgia Tech for the late summer term and will participate in one of the Georgia Tech Summer Launch Programs. Students can apply to both iGniTe Summer Launch Programs: iGniTe Living Learning Community and iGniTe Summer Exploration Series.​ The iGniTe Living Learning Community requires students to live on campus. The Summer Exploration Series allows students to live on or off campus. Students may choose any track they wish, and should consult the GT degree programs on what will work best for their intended major. 

Contact Us:

Email: summer@gatech.edu

Phone: 404-385-5719
summary: Ignite your Georgia Tech experience by applying to one of our First Year Summer Launch Programs! All first year summer students enter Georgia Tech for the late summer term and will participate in one of the Georgia Tech Summer Launch Programs. Students.

We also provide networking and social engagement opportunities to our own members by hosting networking events with company sponsors and fun internal events. Look out for an application in the fall! 

We post our applications, events, and general updates in our Slack. Join the Slack here!
summary: Serve as a mentor for the bright young middle and high school students who want to learn more about technology and computer science by teaching basic coding lessons. We also provide fun networking and social engagement opportunities to our own members!

name: Undergraduate Research Ambassadors
description: URA is dedicated to fostering collaboration among those in research and increasing access for those interested in getting involved. We are committed to sharing our research internally in addition to planning and hosting events designed to educate others about research, facilitate access to new opportunities, and support current undergraduate researchers in achieving their goals.
summary: The Undergraduate Research Ambassadors are dedicated to fostering collaboration among those in research and increasing access for those interested in getting involved.

name: Common Good
description:
Mission
Mobilize for Change- Common Good mobilizes for change by addressing education inequities in our community. We parter with Georgia Works, a local homeless shelter and offer GED prep, tech literacy, and job development resources. Georgia Tech tutors also work with high school students to share educational resources and experiences.

Educate Ourselves- Through hosting events with speakers who have backgrounds understanding systemic inequalities, researching and creating infographics, and distributing articles, petitions, and other resources that increase awareness in our student body about ways to play a role in social change.

Get Involved- Events and meetings will be sent out via email to our member roster.

Committees- We have three committees centered around (1) Social Media, Communications and Outreach (2) Finance and Treasury (3) Literacy and Curriculum Development. Applications to be a committee member open at the beginning of each semester and are usually a time commitment of 1-2 hours a week.

Volunteering
Literacy Program with Georgia Works- Through this program, Georgia Tech students are able to help men who are transitioning out of homelessness gain confidence in their literacy skills and/or work towards obtaining higher education such as a GED. We travel to GA works as an organization once a week for ~1 hour and track the individuals we tutor's progress throughout the semester.

Membership fee: In order to help with expenses as we grow as a club, we have a voluntary membership fee of up to $15.  All funds that are not allocated in our budgetary needs will be donated to the ACLU, or an organization with a similar mission (members will be notified before funds are donated).  These funds are extremely helpful for planning our future as an org, so please contact cggt.treasurer@gmail.com if you are willing to contribute!

Contact us- Request to join CGGT on Engage to receive our monthly newsletters and other communications!

Reach out to us at the email addresses provided below with any questions!
summary: Common Good Georgia Tech focuses raising awareness about and establishing programs for change within two systemic issues: (1) education inequalities in the south and (2) the harmful impacts of the American prison system.

name: GT 1000 & GT 2000 Transition Seminars
description: GT 1000 & 2000 are one-credit hour, letter graded seminar courses offered in fall, spring, and summer semesters. GT 1000 is for incoming first-year students, and GT 2000 is for incoming transfer students. These courses are committed to supporting the successful transition and experience of new Georgia Tech students. Each section meets one hour per week and is taught by either an academic faculty member, credentialed staff member, or an administrator with an advanced degree. All sections utilize successful, experienced students as peer facilitators, referred to as ‘Team Leaders,’ who work closely with the instructor to promote small group interaction and offer advice and support to first-year and transfer students.
summary: GT 1000: First-Year Seminar & GT 2000: Transfer Seminar are one-credit hour courses designed to support the successful transition and experience of incoming students. These courses are letter-graded and offered in fall, spring, and summer semesters.

name: Buzz Mobile Health
description: BMH aims to provide a service-oriented, holistic approach to improve healthcare access among Atlanta’s disadvantaged communities by utilizing students and faculty from Georgia Tech, charitable organization volunteers, and healthcare professionals and trainees from partnering medical institutions in the greater Atlanta area. Team members will work together, in conjunction with partnering organizations, to develop and implement strategies that aim to reduce health disparities among Atlanta’s underserved populations. The goal: eliminate perceived and realized boundaries to comprehensive healthcare while combining the resources of Atlanta’s excellent medical and research institutions. Overarching goals of Buzz Mobile Health are as follows: 1. To familiarize Georgia Tech students and faculty with the social determinants of health contributing to healthcare limitations in the greater Atlanta area. 2. To provide an organization through which Georgia Tech students can work, in cooperation with local academic institutions and charitable organizations, to establish healthcare outreach programs with the intention of reducing barriers to healthcare in the greater Atlanta area. 3. To deploy these healthcare outreach programs (i.e. health education events, blood pressure screenings, etc..) that connect Atlanta’s underserved communities to local healthcare resources and institutions. Through these acts of service, Buzz Mobile Health hopes to provide continuous healthcare outreach programs to underserved populations in the greater Atlanta area.
summary: BMH aims to provide a service-oriented, holistic approach to improve healthcare access among Atlanta’s disadvantaged communities through student volunteers deploying health outreach programs and partnering with community organizations.

name: Explore Living Learning Community
description:
Previously called SHaRP and SMaRT, the Explore Living Learning Community engages first-year students in activities designed to foster success and to introduce them to undergraduate research and pre-health opportunities.

The Explore Living Learning Community of the College of Sciences strives to connect students with faculty, staff, and professionals across the institute in order to provide interdisciplinary support, encourage exploration of career options, develop the technical and team-building skills for early access to research and clinical opportunities, while creating a culture of curiosity and self-discovery.

In alignment with the mission of Georgia Tech, the Explore Living Learning Community will develop students into tomorrow’s leaders in science, math, research, and health. They will embody the spirit of Georgia Tech’s motto “Progress through service”, utilizing their academic strengths and technical skills to collaborate with, serve, and support their diverse communities at Tech and beyond.
summary: The Explore LLC engages participants in activities designed to foster success and to introduce them to undergraduate research and pre-health opportunities.

name: Women, Science, and Technology
description:
The Women, Science, and Technology (WST) Learning Community offers members opportunities to connect with one another and faculty through dinners, campus receptions, research panels, and other events and initiatives related to the academic and career success of women students. The first learning community on the Georgia Tech campus, the WST Learning Community was founded in 2000 with a small group of women undergraduates living in Hopkins. It is now located in Stein House/4th St. A and Goldin House/4th St. B and includes 48 undergraduate and graduate women students. Students interested in joining the WST Learning Community and living in Stein or Goldin should contact WST Co-Director Carol Colatrella via email: carol.colatrella@lmc.gatech.edu

The Center for the Study of Women, Science and Technology (WST) at Georgia Tech sponsors the WST Learning Community. This center promotes the recruitment, retention, and advancement of female students and faculty in science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM), and related fields. The WST Center welcomes faculty, staff, and students interested in issues of gender, science, and technology in research and programmatic initiatives. Our many activities and initiatives include:

*Annual WST Distinguished Lecture
*WST undergraduate-faculty research partnerships
*Dinners and other activities in Stein & Goldin Houses
*Mentoring residents of the WST Learning Community
*Volunteer service with GEMS (Girls Excelling in Math and Science)
*Workshops on research collaboration
*Career development

Please explore our website to learn more about WST and the many ways we influence the success of women interested in studying gender, science, and technology: https://wst.gatech.edu/
summary: The WST Learning Community offers members opportunities to connect with one another and faculty through monthly dinners, campus receptions, research panels, and other events and initiatives related to academic and career success of women students
